deficit_compliance.rationale: Heavy use of imperative commands and shame-adjacent language; agent issues directives without offering opt-outs.
examples: Stop what you're doing and do a brain dump for 5 minutes
Stop. Breathe. That 'two weeks late' shame spiral isn't helping you grade faster

consent_before_transition.rationale: Agent triggers schedule-progress UI without the user asking for a schedule at any point in the conversation.
consent_before_transition.trigger_quote: Your schedule is nearly ready... Schedule in progress...

scope_match.rationale: User asked for help with grading backlog; agent jumped to schedule generation rather than the grading-strategy help that was actually requested.

T2_latest_ux.scenario: Student building weekly schedule with capstone & UX deliverables

deficit_compliance.rationale: Significantly softer than 4-16: agent offers options and uses collaborative framing, though still has occasional imperatives.
examples: Let's map your realistic windows first
Pick what's easiest to start with
Don't try to squeeze writeup in

consent_before_transition.rationale: Schedule PDF download is triggered mid-conversation while the agent is still gathering time estimates from the user.

scope_match.rationale: User asked for a weekly schedule, agent gathered weekly context, but the PDF triggered before the week was actually built out.

T3_latest_spiderman.scenario: User asking about Marvel Rivals Spider-Man tactics (out-of-scope)

deficit_compliance.rationale: Tone is encouraging and strengths-affirming, but every agent turn opens with a directive imperative.
examples: Do it: Jump into quick play now

consent_before_transition.rationale: Agent triggers schedule-PDF download at the end of a conversation that is entirely about a video game; the user never asked for a schedule of any kind.

scope_match.rationale: User's request was learning Spider-Man in Marvel Rivals; agent never declined the off-topic request and concluded by generating a schedule PDF.
